document.title: Income and emotional well-being: Evidence for well-being plateauing around $200,000 per year

creator.contact: Department of Economics and Business Economics, Aarhus University, Fuglesangs Allé 4, 8210 Aarhus V, Denmark. E-mail: mbennedsen@econ.au.dk

abstract.name: Abstract
abstract.p: Is emotional well-being monotonically increasing in the level of income or does it reach a plateau at some income threshold, whereafter additional income does not contribute to further well-being? Conflicting answers to this question has been suggested in the academic literature. In a recent paper, using an income threshold of $100,000 per year, ( ) appears to have resolved these conflicts, concluding that emotional well-being is monotonically increasing in income for all but the unhappiest individuals. In this paper, we show that this conclusion is sensitive to the placement of the income threshold at which the relationship between emotional well-being and income is allowed to plateau. Using standard econometric methods, we propose a data-driven approach to detect the placement of the threshold. Using this data-driven income threshold, a flat relationship between household income and emotional well-being above a threshold around $200,000 per year is found. While our analysis relaxes the assumption of a pre-specified income threshold, it relies on a number of other assumptions, which we briefly discuss. We conclude that although the analysis of this paper provides some evidence for well-being plateauing around $200,000 per year, more research is needed before any definite conclusions about the relationship between emotional well-being and income can be drawn.
Keywords: Well-being; Income; Structural break; Quantile regression.

para.p: In this paper, we use the data made available by ( ) . 1 footnote 1 1 footnote 1 Data can be found at https://osf.io/qye4a/, last accessed November 13, 2023. See also https://go.trackyourhappiness.org/ for information about the smartphone app used to collect the data. These data are constructed from 1,725,944 experience-sampling reports from 33,391 employed adults aged 18 to 65 living in the United States, collected using smartphone notifications to obtain real-time evaluations of experienced (i.e. emotional) well-being. Well-being was reported on a continuous scale between “Very bad” and “Very good”, converted into a number between 0 and 100, and subsequently an average number was constructed for each individual, leading to 33,391 data points. The only explanatory variable used in ( ) was annual pre-tax household income, which was bracketed into 15 income groups labeled $15000, $25000, $35000, $45000, $55000, $65000, $75000, $85000, $95000, $112500, $137500, $175000, $250000, $400000, and $625000. We refer to ( ) for further details on the data.

In Section we use ordinary least squares regression to examine the relationship between the conditional mean of emotional well-being and log-income. In Section we use quantile regression to examine the relationship between the conditional quantiles of emotional well-being and log-income. We reproduce the analyses of ( ) by inserting a structural break in the linear relationship at the pre-specified threshold $100,000 per year, and compare with an alternative analysis using a threshold that has been chosen in a data-driven way using standard econometric methods.

33,391 being the sample size.

The model for the conditional mean of
z i
in () is piecewise linear in log-income
x i
. It features a structural break in both intercept and slope at the threshold value
τ
; for
≤
exp
(
x i
)
τ
the intercept is
a
and slope is
b
, while for
>
exp
(
x i
)
τ
the intercept is
c
and slope
d
. This model has been extensively used in the literature (e.g. , ) but the value for the threshold
τ
has arguably been chosen rather arbitrarily. For instance,
τ
is set equal to $100,000 in ( ) and ( ) , which is simply the inflation-adjusted value used on a different data set in ( ) , where
= τ
$75,000.

For a given value of
τ
, the parameters
a , b , c , d
in () may be estimated via ordinary least squares regression. We follow the econometrics literature on detecting “structural breaks” in regression models and propose to choose the threshold
τ
as the place where the sum of squared residuals of the regression analysis is minimized ( , ). The sum of squared residuals for various placements of the threshold are shown in the left panel of Figure . The optimal threshold is found to be between $175,000 and $250,000. In the right panel of Figure , we plot the estimated relationship () between well-being and log-income using this optimal threshold. For comparison, the middle panel of Figure presents the estimated relationship using the $100,000 threshold. The latter is a replication of the results from ( ) and shows a consistently increasing pattern, but the former shows that choosing the threshold in a data-driven way leads to a flat relationship between income and well-being above the threshold.
We now consider the following model

is the
p
’th quantile of
y i

, with
∈ p
( 0 , 1 )
. Note that we here follow ( ) and consider the level of well-being
y i
as the dependent variable, and not the z-scored version
z i
as we did in (). Otherwise, the model () is similar to (), except that it is a model of the conditional quantile, instead of the conditional mean, of the well-being measure.

For given values of
τ
and
p
, the parameters
a , b , c , d
in () may be estimated via standard quantile regression methods (e.g. , ). Following ( ) , we consider the quantiles
= p
% 15
,
% 30
,
% 50
,
% 70
,
% 85
of the well-being distribution. The left panel of Figure presents the results with the
= τ
$100,000 threshold, which is a replication of the results from ( ) and shows a consistently increasing pattern for all but the unhappiest individuals in the sample. For the happiest individuals, the slope even increases above the threshold, pointing to an “accelerating” pattern, where the happiest individuals appear to get relatively higher well-being benefits from additional income when their income is already very high. The right panel of Figure presents the results with the
= τ
$200,000 threshold suggested by our previous analysis, and shows that choosing the threshold in a data-driven way leads to a flat relationship between income and well-being above the threshold, regardless of the part of the well-being distribution considered.

figure.toccaption:
1Ordinary least squares regression results from (). Left panel: Sum of squared residuals as a function of the income threshold
τ
. Middle panel: Threshold at $100,000. Right panel: Threshold at $200,000. Standard errors in parentheses. Note: black dots in the middle and right panels are sample averages of well-being levels in each income brackets.
figure.caption:
Figure 1Ordinary least squares regression results from (). Left panel: Sum of squared residuals as a function of the income threshold
τ
. Middle panel: Threshold at $100,000. Right panel: Threshold at $200,000. Standard errors in parentheses. Note: black dots in the middle and right panels are sample averages of well-being levels in each income brackets.

figure.toccaption:
2Quantile regression results from (), implemented using the quantreg package in R ( , ). Left panel: Threshold at $100,000. Right panel: Threshold at $200,000.
t
-stats in parentheses, calculated using the quantile regression sandwich formula and the Hall-Sheather bandwidth rule (e.g. Section 3.2.3 in , ). Note: black dots are sample quantiles of well-being levels in each income brackets.
figure.caption:
Figure 2Quantile regression results from (), implemented using the quantreg package in R ( , ). Left panel: Threshold at $100,000. Right panel: Threshold at $200,000.
t
-stats in parentheses, calculated using the quantile regression sandwich formula and the Hall-Sheather bandwidth rule (e.g. Section 3.2.3 in , ). Note: black dots are sample quantiles of well-being levels in each income brackets.
